This is Yonsei. OK. So one of the common themes that we get in terms of, yeah, I'm not even taking a breath. We're going right into it. So one of the common questions that we get all the time is about partnerships and about operations managers and how to handle them, how to pay them, all of those questions. And so we wanted to break down the things that have worked well for us so that you can take them. And a lot of the beliefs that we now have, given the growth that we've had, that I didn't know the things that I know now in terms of how to run a business. And Layla knows way more than I do about how to run a business because she runs ours. And so she's going to talk about her expertise. And I will try and give it to you from a gym owner's perspective, because a lot of you guys are male, not everyone, but I'm saying, like, me as a husband and me as a gym owner, some of the beliefs that I had to adopt in order to make this work better. That makes sense. OK. So we're going to try and go over four things. First one is the first follower theory. We'll go over a brief, like, our story, and we'll try and make it relevant to why this will apply for you. The second will be the traits of the leader. Third is going to be the traits of the first follower. And the fourth is going to be the dynamic between the two of them and how it works. And that'll be part one. Part two is how to build an unshakable partnership off of that dynamic. The third will be why partnerships fail. Basically, it's a do list of what not to do. Many things I've done. And how to use this information if you're a solo operator. So who here is a solo operator who doesn't have a wife involved or a spouse involved? Interesting. Cool. No, actually, I thought it was going to be more split, but we have more couples. That's cool. That's really cool. OK. So here's what we want you to leave from this. We want to leave knowing how to create and find your first follower, because those are twofold. When Laila and I first met, she wasn't the first follower at that moment. We both had to create this dynamic that's created the growth that we've had. The second is how to build a great partnership or fix a not so great one. So you have the actual steps of what to do. OK. Important note, for some of you, this may be your spouse. For others, this may be someone that you need to hire. So for those of you who just raised your hand, this may be someone that doesn't actually exist in your organization right now. And I can tell you that I know that if I had had this person, I had glimpses of this at different points when I had the gym. But now that I know the ideal scene of what it should have looked like, this is what I would have done, and this is what you should do. For others, you may need to take steps to improve your existing, quote, partnership arrangement. But it does not mean that I'm recommending you take a partner on. So just to be clear here, thank you, Jeff. Appreciate that. So I'm not saying, hey, I'm a solo operator. I need to give half of my gym away. Or like, hey, there's this fun client investor who wants to give me 50 grand for half my gym. Like, that's not what I'm talking about. It doesn't necessarily mean that they have to be equals in equity. It's just in terms of partnership of how you're approaching the business. I would keep talking if we didn't have those slides. It's true. It's happened before. OK, awesome. So first, before, I don't know which button clicks. Oh, green. Green. OK. So before we go into talking about the first follower theory, how many of you know what that is? Or I've seen that video. Derek Silver's viral. OK, cool. One person. Relevant. So the first follower theory is the concept of attracting an adherent to some sort of viewer initiative as the first step to beginning a movement that might seem unusual or out of step with the ordinary. So when you talk about an adherent, you're actually talking about that follower or that person. And then the movement is the concept. So essentially, that person becomes proof of concept. So some examples of this that Alex and I were talking about. Elon Musk and Gwen Shotwell, Steve Jobs and Steve Wozniak, Ben and Jerry's, Tony Stark and Pepper Potts. So you see it all over. We were talking about it when we went to see the superhero movie that just came out. Yes. And there's so many of those pairs in there, because this is something that's so common. And so if any of you have seen the video of Derek Silver's that he wrote on, it's basically this guy that's in the middle of a field. And he starts dancing like a crazy person. And he looks crazy until one person joins him. And that one person then starts a movement in which everybody else that's sitting around doing absolutely nothing then joins them, because that one person did something very important. They confirmed to everybody else watching that leader is not crazy or stupid, but they're actually worthy of following. And that was exactly what I did for Alex when we met. So people didn't think he's crazy. So why should you care about having your first follower? One, it's going to make your viewpoint seem much more credible, because a lot of the time, the only difference between what someone would consider crazy and someone would consider intelligent or genius is that people believe in the person that other people would call genius. People believe in Alex. He was just going around the street talking about this gym stuff, and nobody followed him. It would be like, OK, he's crazy. Second is because it takes away the risk for everybody else. And once you have your first follower, it takes away the risk for everyone else to join on and hop on the bandwagon, because what was weird becomes cool. And then lastly, so this is an interesting one. This person is going to set the foundation for your entire culture of your business, because other people in the business are going to mimic your first follower rather than the leader. So what you have in your business right now, if you think your culture sucks, or whether you think your culture is strong, it's probably because of how the first person, whoever your right hand is, is to you. So a side note is Alex was going over our seven-figure gym owners and who won that for this event. And this wasn't surprising to us, but 95% of them, I think it was all except one, had this exact dynamic. They had, and it was so clear to us, I mean, they're like these super strong people that work together. It doesn't mean they're necessarily married, but it means they had a partner. And so that kind of brought me to this quote, which is, growth is not by mere chance, it is the power of forces working together. And so a lot of us get into business because we don't like how other people operate, or we feel like we're different, or we need to do our things our own way, and we don't want to follow rules and all this stuff. But if you ever want people to work together and to actually build a business, you have to be able to set rules and then stick by them and join forces with other people. And it's almost like you then become accountable to them. And something you can't change your mind every day. You can't change your mind. It was a business all the time. You have to stick with the things that you put in place. So to understand how you guys can apply this to, we'll tell you a little bit about our story. So we just went to see Star Wars, so we put this in. So a long time ago in a galaxy far away, just a little bit of context. We met on Bumble. So who here has seen Bumble? Anyone? It's class year tinder. Yeah, it's class year tinder. So I think it was like our first mastermind, Alex talked about it, the Bumble funnel. So it was like a joke because essentially our first date, he tells me how he got me in there because it was a funnel. And then we ended up literally talking about business for like four hours. And so we walked in circles. And as we're walking, he's like, this is a sales tactic because we're walking in circles, which makes you trust me. And so at first, I honestly, I don't know how many of you know this. But we were not boom chemistry or anything like that. But I was like, this dude is freaking smart. And I was drinking the juice that he was pouring. I thought what he had to say. That was so terrible. That's right, baby. OK. I was trying to be like, you get it. So the entire first date was literally talking about marketing, talking about sales, and the fitness industry. And literally, he's pitching me during this date on his idea to take over the world. And so I'm just sitting there. And I'm just like, in my mind, I'm like, he's kind of crazy. But I also think he's genius. And so dates two through 17, literally, I told him after the first date, I was like, we're not going to talk about business. And then all we talked about was business. And so he tells me about client finance acquisition. He's like, you should really work for me. Literally the second date, he's like, you should work for me. I can pay you more. He kept saying the same phrase over and over. He's like, just let me leapfrog you. And I was like, what do you mean? And I kind of want to skip back to this and give you guys context. Why was I so infatuated with what he was saying? Why was it that I was such an ideal avatar for his first follower? Like, why was that? And what do you look for in that person? OK, to start with a little bit of my backstory, I had lost 85 pounds. So I'd gone through college. I'd gained weight, drinking, drugs, all that fun stuff. Gained a bunch of weight. Started working out, lost the weight. Then, of course, everyone, most of you probably, you're like, I want to help other people do this. And so then I started doing that. And then I was like, wow, I freaking hate my life. I'm exhausted all the time. And training people one on one is literally cutting grass with scissors. I hate this, right? And I'm exhausted. And I think the worst thing you can do for your fitness is getting into fitness, especially one on one. I also realized that all of my years of exercise science and exercise physiology and deciding of was I going to go to master's school, or was I going to go and try and get a job. And then I get out. And then I was like, oh my gosh, none of this matters, right? Because all that actually matters is do you know how to market it and do you know how to sell? So I was at the point where I had gotten out of school. I had spent all this money and all this time learning proper technique and form. And if you turn this joint this way, this happens. And the biomechanics of the body and this and the cue angle on your hip. And then they get out. And I go and I get a job at 24-hour fitness. And I was like, oh my god, it really doesn't matter. What I have going for me is that I'm a decent-looking girl in California. And I'm learning how to talk to people, which is sell, right? It's just reality. So naturally, as this was where I ended up, I called myself like I was a typical seeker at the moment that I met Alex. So I was in this spot where I had done all this really hard work. And it got me to this place where, yeah, I was doing really well. And I was helping a lot of people in context. But I was like, my gosh, I feel like there's something so broken in this industry. And that was literally my thought. I was like, they don't tell you any of this. Nothing is taught in school that was relevant. They teach you nothing about business. And so I'm sitting there. I remember I would every Sunday do like four hours of Tony Robbins. And I had a dream board, which my sister, Mimi, who's our COO in the back, can I test you? I had that goal board or that dream board that said, I had them broken down by yearly, monthly, daily. I had all my student loan debt plus other debt. I was driving. My parents had given me a Prius with a crack and a dent. And I was like, it doesn't matter because one day I'm going to be rich because I'm going to save my money now and not care what anyone else thinks, right? And I was reading things like the four hour work week because I was like, I'm so exhausted that I'm literally drinking coffee at 7 o'clock, teaching a session, and I'm actually falling asleep while I'm doing this. And I had been in Michigan. And of course, I was like, OK, you can't make dreams come true here, so I have to go to California to do that and just lose all of my money and just burn it, but gain skills. And so that being said, it really didn't take much effort from Alex or much selling. It took him five weeks to convince me to leave absolutely everything that I had built in California and then go and join him in our new home together, which was the extended stay. Right. Like, that's the state. So you guys have to understand, taking someone I had never met him before, I didn't know who this dude was, and then five weeks later, I'm calling up my sister, I'm calling up my family, and I'm like, guys, I'm going to quit everything. And we're going to start this thing called gym launch. And my dad, I just remember, was like, you're so compulsive. And so you fast forward 26 months from now, from then. And this relationship has really been the single best thing that's happened to either of us. And what most of you don't know, unless you've talked to us a little bit more in depth, is that it really did start as more of business partners and then romantic second, because we started working together so quickly. And in such a, I want to say, high stress setting that all we saw off the bat was like, we're in an extended stay, we're selling for 12 hours a day together, he's like, you need to make more calls, you need to do this, you need to do that. And I'm like sitting here like, go off to the bathroom, like dad, this sucks. I'm like, you know? But the strengths from our relationship really do come from our business side and from having this dynamic that we built together, rather than our romantic side. In fact, the cool thing is that you can take everything in business and that you have on that side of your relationship, you can apply it to the romantic side because I really do think, and this is something that both Alex and I believe, is that it's like, why? You can create romance, like it's built. Everything takes effort. Regardless, why are we telling you this? So that you can model the successful pieces of our partnership where it's appropriate. Because when we started off together, we both had that like burning desire and that mission, but it really takes effort on both ends. And so what you might be in right now or what you might be looking for, it might not be perfect right now or what you're looking for, it will not be perfect when you find it, but you can make it freaking awesome. So we're gonna go over the traits of the leader, the traits of the first follower, and the traits of the relationship. So I will explain the traits of the leader. So what attracted me to Alex? Like what did he do so well that it persuaded me to become his follower? So first off, clear vision of the future. I think this is one that anyone who talks to Alex on like a daily basis or anyone in his little circle knows. Because he will talk about the, like all he talks about is the future. He lives in the future. And so from the moment I met him, he's telling me, he has six gyms, so I'm like, you have six gyms. And instead he's not talking about those gyms, he's talking about like, here's where we're going, here's what we're gonna do, here's how we're gonna do it. And like it's so clear because he's talked about it so many times in like in-depth detail that it was, it's like you can't ignore it. Second, I don't even need to explain that one, he's persuasive and charismatic. That's obvious. He's a giver in all aspects. So that's something that when I met him, I would see how much he would give in excess to people of what he took back from them. And that's something I think he's kept to this day and he always does in any relationship. He always makes sure that he's given an abundance. Competitive and somebody who's going to be a leader, like when I look for a leader because I've, I've been under people who I would consider or you know, whatever went into their company and I would think they're not a great leader because they almost weren't competitive enough. He is very competitive, but not too much that he will let it trump what is right for the company and the customer, right? Extremely growth oriented. So he's always looking to do something new and every aspect of his life, he's always looking to grow and become more and he moves super, super, super, super fast. Intolerant of the normal standard. So I would say these last two actually are probably the two most important. He's intolerant of normal standards. So when I say, well, this is just how it is. Like even with us today in the business, he's like, well, that's not acceptable. Like there is another way. And I mean, if anyone's ever told Alex something can't be done, they will watch him literally break it down by math on a whiteboard and prove to you why it can be done, right? And then being insanely high expectations of himself and others. So the interesting part that you guys should really be thinking about is that someone like Alex who has such high expectations of himself is going to repel people who have low expectations of themselves and instead for people like me when I'm like brutal on myself, I am attracted to something like that. So if you're looking for people who wanna follow you, you shouldn't be making it look like what you do is easy because hard workers are attracted to people who work hard, right? Alex. And just as an aside, something that's not on the slide. Thank you, Leila. Is that like I can't express in words how much this relationship. And we're really only talking about it from a business standpoint. Like the reason that we have been as successful as we have and the reason that we have grown as quickly, like we're not the first gym gurus, right? Like we're not. But the reason that we've been able to scale deliverable, like scale service, scale deliverability, scale customer experience to such a high degree is because of the dynamic of this relationship. And I'm gonna say something and I hope it doesn't come off air again. I really hope, I hope it doesn't. I think that we have one of the best partnerships that I've seen. And I think that we're really gonna, I think this is gonna be a lot bigger than it is right now, just being honest with you. And everything that we're sharing is stuff that's like right now, like as we're in the thick of it. And I think like there's just, it's like I wish I could jump into my mind as a gym owner and like explain to myself how important this is. And I'd heard talks that were not gonna be as tactical as this about how important treating your employees are, how important building a team is, how important culture is. And I was always like, whatever, you gotta sell shit. And I'm telling you, like it's the secret. It's the, like it's our secret sauce. Like Layla is our secret sauce, the dynamic we have. The reason that I stay in my little three person bubble is because of the dynamic that we have. And just being radically self aware I can manage very little. I'm like, I get overwhelmed very easily. I get tons of anxiety when I have too many people talking to me. That's why we created a fortress and I live at home and I don't like, I wake up and go to my gym and I like go back to my zoom call and like I stay in my house and I'm like, I'm good. And Layla is an amazing leader of people and I don't know if you guys can see that but she leads by such example. And so let me tell you some of the traits of the first follower. And we should all be so lucky as to find a Layla. So dedicated to the mission. So that's the first thing is that I, when I was like, listen, I want to fix the fitness industry. I was really talking about the industry in general and she was working at a micro gym. So it totally resonated with her. She's like, they totally need help. They're like barely making it. At the time she'd known the owner closely because he had taken her from 24, which I recommend you guys do go to the big gyms and take the best people. He had taken her from 24 and she literally cut her pay in half or more to go join his gym. So like people are willing to work for less to have just a different environment that's not 24-hour fitness. But she was dedicated when I was like, this is how we can fix this. Like this is how it works. Like we just have to teach this to as many people as we can. And she just believed in that. And so that was the first piece. So if you have somebody who doesn't care about Layla, doesn't care about customers, like it's just not gonna work and you have to sense that. Some of the stuff that's in here, I try to balance this with the super tactical stuff. This is more emotional intelligence, but we're trying to make this as step by step as we can. The second aspect is that she's protective of me. She really protects me a lot, which is interesting because I'm the man in our relationship and she's the woman, but within the business, she protects me in my time and my space, I don't know, like a tiger, Tigris, whatever. She's ferocious about it. Like if anybody in our company messages me directly, they're gonna have a one-on-one with Layla and she's gonna explain to them why they should never do that. And it's gonna be a serious issue. And she's done this a lot of times because she protects that space so much because if I get agitated, everyone feels it. And we as entrepreneurs tend to be more sensitive and it's a gift and a curse. And that's why you see all these billionaires who are like Richard Branson bought a freaking island to just keep, just like have his space, you know what I mean? And so like we have to like, we can't all afford an island, but we can make our own islands in terms of how we're managing our attention and our time and our space and who we're surrounding ourselves with and the people that have access to us, which is also controlling that communication flow that comes to you. Third, she's long-term minded, which is important. You wanna make sure that this kind of goes hand in hand with the first thing like, they need to be bought into the mission, they need to be seen, this is like, yes, we're gonna work hard. And like it's not, it's gonna like, when you're stacking EFTs, you're like, we're gonna get there, five more, five more, like another hour of calls, like we can do it. You know what I mean? You have to have someone who can see where you're going and can see it as clearly as you can. Fourth thing is that she is really, really, really decisive, which means that like I don't have to make, like she was the first duplication of decision-making that we had. I can trust Layla for making decisions 100% of the time and she is going to mess up because she's human and I probably would have messed up worse if I had been in her situation. And so the first thing is like, you can't, like that person needs to be able to make decisions without you and be independent and you need to give them that independence and we'll talk about that in a little bit more. Fifth was, Layla kind of balances me, I don't know if you guys have seen that, if you can like see the dynamic. She's far more steady and patient than I am, by a lot. A lot. But that steadiness is really what's necessary for the team to grow. Our team would not have grown to where it is without Layla because she's like the rock of our whole company. I'm like the fire, well we'll talk about that in a second, but it's so important to have that counterbalance because what she was saying with the first followers, that grounds your beliefs and everything that you're about to say, cause you're like, okay, well if Layla thinks it's like, yeah, sure Alex wants to start a rocket ship, but like what does Layla think? Layla thinks it's a good idea, okay. What is it again? Have you ever heard people like this? Or you're like, okay, that could be a good idea, and then you hear from the other person, you're like, okay, now I'm gonna actually give you my attention and actually think about it. Most people won't even give you that first thing, so if any of you guys have culture issues, or like my trainers don't listen to me, it's because you're lacking that. Sixth was, she's extremely coachable. I'll just tell you within our relationship, in the very beginning she used to laugh, and some people do this, she's like, you're stupid. She'd laugh and say that. And I remember I was like, can you not say that? I was like, it just bothers me. And she was like, okay, never said it again, from that point on. She was somebody who could change on a dime, and it was immediate. And so if you have someone that rare unicorn, who you don't even need to repeat something that you need to say, like hey, can you change this? And then it's just changed, it's mind blowing to watch. But it's a huge trait that has been unbelievably valuable to us. She's extremely discerning, she's very good at interpersonal like, well this person's this, this person said this, what do I do? She's just wise. And think about King Solomon, that ability to discern situations, like what's the truth here? Like how do we get to the bottom of this? You wanna have someone who has a very high emotional intelligence, so that they can navigate those situations without blowing you up. Because those are the things, all the things that she deals with on a regular basis would totally destroy me. A gym owner who for some reason can't get leads, breaks my heart and I can't think about it for two days and I just can't sleep and it's horrible. And it is a really big problem, but I can't do that or the business will crash. You know what I mean? So she has to protect that. And the last is that, this is more specific to Layla, but if you can find someone like Hesis, it's great. She's a psychopath. She's a robot, she has more discipline than anyone I've ever met. Almost to the point where I joked about her, I was like, I think you almost enjoy suffering. You can just like, she just change, cause it goes with the coaching thing, like she can just change course on a dime and then like go and it's never, she never looks back and she's just all in always. And if you can find even half of these traits in someone, especially if it's not your spouse, but if it's someone who's working for you, so I mean operations manager, this is the list that I would look for. Okay, so the fun part, the dynamic. So first off, you need to establish who is who. So who here has two people in your business? Who here doesn't know who's who of what we just went over? Okay, no, it's good. So here's your barometer, here's your checklist. All right, and this will kind of reinforce some of the things. One person, here's like 12 metaphors. One person's gonna be more of the gas, the other person's more of the brakes. I'm like, hey, let's do this tomorrow. And she's like, what? Slow down, like maybe, but we have to build a whole department and that won't, like we have to hire six people, put training and onboarding, we have to make client contracts and they're like, but can't we just do it? So whichever part of that conversation you think you would be, if you were the me in that one, then you'd be more leader, if you'd be more heard, then you get it. One person fuels the ideas, the other person says, yes, but how? One person tends to be more persuasive and charismatic and the other tends to be more level-headed and grounded. I'm not saying that the person can't sell. Layla broke my record. So really Layla's just better than me on most things. But we have tendencies, like I tend to be more persuasive, charismatic, she tends to be more level-headed and grounded. It doesn't mean that she can't sell or can't have that skill set. They can question, push and challenge each other without questioning intent. That's kind of more of a dynamic thing, not an either or. But you have to be able to respect that person's, like if she says no, I don't just say, well, tough shit, we're doing it anyways. And some of you guys know that you've done that, because I have, plenty of times. And that was in the beginning and now we don't do that as much and that's why we've grown. Which is where the whole being bored, like you just gotta get used to it. That's something that you have to evolve. The follow protects the leader, above all else, extreme ownership. So if you feel like one of you kind of plays defense to the other, that's those, one person is more charismatic leader, the other person is more first follower. One focuses on the vision, the other focuses on getting there. So one's in the clouds, one's in the dirt. Does anyone not know which one they are? And if you don't know, you can decide. Actually, you wanna say how we did with Charfin? Oh, I mean it's really simple. Yes, so it was really easy. So we literally just sat in a room and wrote the functions of the business, like finance, legal, HR, sales, marketing, customer service. And then we took Alex and put him under marketing. And then the rest of the board was Layla. It was the best consulting day ever. I was like, you can handle that. Yeah, and we have more of that described in detail and some stuff I made for you guys. But I think if anything, when it comes to division of labor, the hardest part is wanting to get in and physically help the person do the work when you see that they're overloaded. I don't get in and help Alex actually do the work for any part. I will help him if he needs to add members to his team. And if I see anything from that standpoint, but I think what I have heard from a lot of you is like there's unclear division or like you're both still doing parts of the same thing. And the difficulty there is that there's, when there's more than one person accountable, like we all know on our team, like before we, for example, before we bought Brittany on for HR, we were all like, man, there's like six of us doing HR right now. And then when everyone's accountable, it's like actually nobody's accountable. And the same thing goes for when there's two people because that puts you in like the perfect position to pick against each other on it. So basically you just divide it. And yeah, I guess, is that what you wanted me to get at? The marketing versus the operations? Yeah, and when we started out, Layla, so this may be familiar to some of you guys, especially those of you who have had a spouse who just left their job and you're like, how do I incorporate them into the gym? That kind of thing. When Layla started working with me, she was like Alex's helper. That was kind of like how the vibe was for the first few months of us working together, which was a gross underutilization of Layla. Massive. And the biggest change that happened was when we had that consulting day, Alex just wrote under her name, COO. And that's when literally everything in our business changed. Because at that point she was like, I'm COO? She's like, oh, well shit, we gotta do all this stuff differently then. I know, right now it's great. And so you have to give that to that person. And it's like, who, Craig, Craig and Nicole. There you are. So Nicole's now in the business. We're both owners. You know what I mean? We're both, it's our company, it's not mine, and Layla helps me. Or like, this is Alex. If she would have come into the gym business with me, she would have taken over operations of all six. You know what I mean? That's what would have happened and then I would have just focused on making it rain. Because that's what I do. But that's how the division of roles works. And the reality is that she does, I mean, we had the board with six things. She had five of them. You know what I mean? It doesn't need to be equal in terms of how many things they're doing. Operations is always gonna do more than the person who is the charismatic leader. And that's usually because I can't do a lot of stuff. Like useless in a lot of ways. So anyways, I just wanted to reinforce that. Okay, so quick recap. First follower theory. You got our story. Hopefully that was useful for you to hear. For those of you who are married, if those of you are not, I'm gonna tie it into like how to do this with operations. Trades to the leader, you should know who you are. Trades to the follower, you should know who you are and how the dynamic sort of works. So what's next is creating an unshakable partnership. Oh, shit. Well, here, do you want to take it or do you want me to take it? No! No! No! No! This is how I always take it. No, I had all this, I was just trying to make it. I was just trying to make it, I was just trying to make it. Okay. Every time. Okay, so steps to creating an unshakable partnership. There's a lot of things, obviously, it's hard to just be like, what makes an, you know, there's a million things, but if we had to like really break it down to three main things, it's equality, clarity, and trust. And so the first one is equality, and that's kind of comes down to what I just said with the owner and the partner and names matter, titles matter, it was something that I was like, whatever, she can call herself the queen of sheba if she wants to, yeah, that's stupid. Like that's dumb, it's a new entrepreneur. It's a new entrepreneur's thinking, like everyone needs to have titles because they care about titles and titles matter. When we changed it from Layla's Alex's Helper to Layla's COO, the game changed. If you want to have your assistant manager take more responsibility, just call her operations manager. And they're like, oh, oops, like okay, I'm operations manager, like it matters. And they have to be seen in your eyes, and you have, like this is something that had to happen for me, so. November of last year, I came in, and I was like, it just enraged almost. And I was like, you're stealing my business from me. I was like, you're taking it from me. Like no joke, this is like kitchen counter. Because I had gone into that meeting, and then I told one of our customer support reps to do something for me, and then she went to another meeting and was in tears and was like, oh my God, Alex told me to do something, I can't do all this stuff. And then they sequestered me, and were like, you can't talk to anyone. Like you have to stay here in this corner, and this was before I had my three man team, I had like a one person team, and they were like, it's just me. They were like, don't talk to anyone, talk to Layla if you wanna talk to people. And so it was incredibly, not humiliating, what's the word, humbling. It was incredibly humbling. But like sometimes you need a break, like Myron said, in order to grow. And so that was my breaking point of realizing that I had to give over some of my power and control to someone else because I couldn't do everything, and she's better at some stuff. And that operations manager should be better than you at most things that have to do with operations, and you have to let them do it. And you have to respect them when they say, hey, we can't do this, and not just overpower them, especially if you're the owner, and they're the operations manager, and you're not spouses, where you're like forced to respect one another. But if you have like, so for example, like Mimi is our COO, and when she and I, like she and I are like polar opposites, like Layla's kind of in the middle, like Mimi and I are like this. And I love you Mimi, it's my sister-in-law. But if she says like, we can't do this yet, I have learned to respect that, because I know that she wants the same thing that I do, and she's looking at it from all the other angles that I'm not. And I just wanna add in. No, but I mean. That should really apply to anybody in your business. So like what I and Alex have dynamic-wise is then reflected in what I and Mimi have, and then is reflected in what she has with her team, and them and their team, and it trickles down. So how you have that relationship and your dynamic with that first follower, the reason that it needs to be so strong and potent, is because like what we have just drips down to the rest of the company, right? And so if you're looking at like, like what he was just talking about with taking someone's opinion, like they say, a lot of the times like I said, if you get into business because you wanna do things your way, well your team, if you are truly respecting them and treating them equally, each person in their role, then you're going to take what they say just as valid as like your own opinion almost, because they have a perspective that you don't. And so if the team says stop, like you can't just ignore them, you have to actually stop. Part two B, clear division of roles. I think we covered this for the most part, but the main takeaway from this slide is that you need to write this stuff down. Because until we wrote it on the board, we could have set it, but once it's defined, then it's concrete. And so we have a rule like even in our company that if we tell someone to do something, we didn't write it down or sign on an email, it didn't happen. Yeah, there's nothing to hold both of you. You have to have like a third party accountability. If you think about religion, when people get married under a religion, it's because there's a third party holding them accountable to following these like values and principles, right? And so you need the same thing for your roles. So we defined our four Rs. So we both have four Rs. documents of exactly what we're supposed to be doing. We defined our communication cycle. So we have an actual communication cycle, like as husband and wife, like we know when we talk about certain things, like it's defined and you should have that. I'd highly recommend it with your spouse, but if your spouse isn't the one who's doing the operations, then you should have a highly defined communication cycle with that person. And the third part is kind of like the, put duct tape on your mouth, sit on your hands. It's okay to be bored, just stay in your lane. Like once you've made that decision, you have to stay with it. Because you're gonna have this impulse, if you're like me, you'll have this impulsive desire to go in and break stuff all the time. And just like go put your nose and stuff. And it's just taking a lot of like learning and messing stuff up to be like, they're better at this than I am. Like and I'm like, that's okay. I mean like as organizations grow, like you get more and more specialized with your skillset and you try and continue to grow in what that is. Okay, so do you wanna read all of these or do you wanna? Okay. Okay, so this is, we thought it would be useful for you guys to see what our four Rs. are. This is my actual four Rs. document. This is like what it is. I'll just read the role. For me, it's to create the vision of the company, count for market realities and undertapped opportunities. Be the face of the company, correspond with other high level vendors and affiliates in order to stay on top of the cutting edge of marketing and sales. And ensure gym owners continue to apply to become customers. That's my job. That's actually, that's my job. That's what I do. From a responsibility standpoint, from a tactical standpoint, I have to stay up to date with all the new advertising platforms and changes and like potential opportunities in the advertising space. Dedicate time and attention to discovering the media channels to get leads for you guys. Continue to innovate and solve the next problem. We're bottleneck presenting gym owners in a tactical and step-by-step manner. Some of this should maybe sound familiar. Create content for distribution in free groups. Create marketing materials like ads. Run traffic from various platforms to generate leads. Create information products and trainings that we sell to end users. Ensure processing guidelines are followed. This is actually something that probably will eventually shift over to operations, but for right now I'm still owning that. Develop strategic partnership with vendors or franchisors like Lyndon. I'm just seeing like, hey, can we do a co-op so everyone saves more money? Stuff like that. And then lead my small executive R&D team, the research and development team. That's what I do. That's my job. And then my role. So, president's role, bring the CEO's vision to life within the company's infrastructure. So the goal is the position of the position is to build sustainable culture and scalable training as the company grows to reach the goals set by the CEO. So the president is considered a representative of the staff and their needs within the company to ensure that they can execute on the CEO's vision. While the CEO designs business strategy, the president is in charge of management slash people strategy. So a difference, just for some of you, because you know Mimi is actually our COO. So I was at the time when we split role COO. So now I am president and she is COO. So how we define the difference between what we do is that she is tactical. She is much better with systems and processes than I am. And I am strategic. So when I get on and I talk with any of our team, like the ones you see today, it's talking about something within their role, their goals, what are they struggling with right now. I'm not like, hey, this is how we're gonna track this and that and here's the system for this or this week this happened. It's talking more of overall, how are you feeling in your role, right? So if you're curious, I just wanted to film the blank. Responsibilities, it's hard to read. Designing and overseeing implementation of scalable training systems and line of strategic goals. Because right now we've hit about 50 people. Before we have 100, probably like 20 people ago, we should have scalable training across all departments, right? Which can easily adapt with all the other platforms that are integrated with Gym Launch. Provide strategic leadership to company. Coaching growth within the company, especially to that top executives. Mentorship and growth plan for executives and various leaders in the company. Establishing policy systems, frameworks that build and protect the company. Culture and values. Evaluate retraining systems and coaching may be needed for the company based on a weekly, monthly, quarterly numbers. Overseeing and identify areas of improvement and hiring, firing, training processes of the employees. The one process I'm probably still the most involved in is the hiring because I feel so closely to that. Because I believe strongly that that is why Gym Launch has gotten to where it is, is because of the hiring decisions we have made because we have a great team. Quality control over the company management executives, overseeing proper legal and financial systems are in place. To support growth and protection of company. Example of why I have that right now is because we don't have a director of finance which we're hiring for, right? Trading and implementing what is needed in terms of team management, training, hiring. Overseeing, lean distribution of resources to elicit maximal effectiveness. Overseeing policies and procedures to learn and they align with the company values. Ensure all team members have proper support system in place and goals are aligned with the companies. So one thing I do for an example that's a little more day to day like that is anytime someone enters our company and they're new or right before we hire them, I will talk to them. Whether it be right before they're hired or right after they're hired. And I will talk to them because other people did the whole hiring process. They're like this person's on board, right? And then I talk to them and make sure that their role and where they want to go with their life makes sense with what they're doing right now in our company. And then direct staff including organizational structure, professional development, motivation. Woo, who does more? But hopefully that just the synopsis about this is like, I may get rain on the lead generation side, make sure that we have inquiries and kind of like see where we're going. And then she sees how do we build the infrastructure to make that happen. Which for us, because we're both in service businesses, us and you guys are both in service businesses. In order to scale service, you scale people. Which is culture and training. That's it, those are the two pieces. Okay, third piece is absolutely trust. So we had a quality, we had clarity and then trust. Okay, this one's easy to say hard to do. I don't know why all of mine are number ones but maybe they are all equally important. Number one, it is to surrender all decisions, results and outcomes to the other person. Like you have to surrender the outcomes and the decisions to the other person. So if that person is owning it, you have to trust them to own it. That means that if they mess up, you can't go in and just tell them that they suck and then like you can't do that. Like it breaks trust and then they just sit there and they don't do anything. Because they're like, oh, I'm just gonna mess it up on my failure. You have to be really intentional about this and it's really hard if you're an entrepreneur because you're like, I wanna control everything and it doesn't work, I tried. You have to trust the other person. Second, without trust neither of you can feel safe to try your hardest because you're afraid to fail with the other person down. So like Layla won't think of like innovative ways to solve problems because if I would like come down on her all the time, if she messed something up, not that she does, but I'm saying like, if I were to do that, she'd be afraid of doing it. So I'm instifling her, I'm like, oh, she's not producing but it's my fault, not hers. The third piece is failure should not ruin trust if the character and the effort are present. I think is a really key point. The reason I think spouses work so well is because usually you have the same intention which is to like grow the business and make money but sometimes people have two different ways of doing that. But if you know that that person's intention is right and you know that that person is working hard and if this same thing for an operations manager who's not your spouse, both ways, if you know that both of those things are in, then you should never come down on someone. Like you can maybe walk through the decision making process and see how maybe like, why did you make that decision? And then a lot of times when early on when we had decisions that we didn't like agree on 100% which isn't that common, she would explain, I'd be like, didn't think it that, good call. Yeah, and I think, yeah, I mean honestly, like these same, like the trust part is really relevant to anybody on your team because what I hear a lot is like people being like, well, I just can't find someone to trust. I just can't like trust people this and that. It's like, dude, it's a freaking discipline. So like work harder on it. Like you have to literally go through the actions before you feel the trust because when you first meet someone and bring them on board, if you're bringing them into your company, you're saying that you trust them. And so you're saying, I trust you enough to let me you in my like little circle. So if you're not giving them the room to fail and instead you were just not trusting them from the beginning, it completely just, it makes absolutely no sense to them. So it shows them that you don't trust them. So then they're not going to do, it causes that person to in turn be less trustworthy because they're not going to do their best job because they're afraid of you. Do you guys see what I'm saying? Yes, okay, cool. It's a circle. So there's the recap of quality, clarity and trust. Those are the things that we believe are the the tenants that make an unchakable partnership. And those are the values that you should be taking to the relationships that you have with your employees. And equality, like, I mean, it even goes like, if Lily says, we can't handle this on customer service, she's our director of customer service. So she handles all the customer service for launch and legacy. So she runs the biggest team in gym launch. And if she's like, I'm like, hey, I want to do this new release. She's like, we're like, the whole team's not trained up on it. And like, if we release it, everyone's gonna have questions and our guys aren't gonna be able to answer it well. I have to respect that. And it takes discipline. Like you want to be like, learn faster. You know, I feel like we're becoming more seasoned in as entrepreneurs. And I just want to give you the scars that we have. So you don't have to have them. Okay, phone part steps to a bad partnership. And what to avoid? This is you. Oh, you're the one with the bad partnerships. Oh, great. Okay. So Alex's how to book classic steps to bad partnerships. So have unclear expectations. Where's Lucette? Lucette's my executive assistant. She's like my, she's like glued to my hip. You've probably seen her walking with me. She carries a shank. Yeah, she carries a shank. It's like, you have to write stuff down and I'm still horrible at this. But if you have unclear expectations, then you're gonna have unclear results and then you're gonna get upset that the results sucked. And it's literally always your fault. But that's a great way. And that's why if you're like, hey, I think we have a pretty clear idea of our roles. You probably don't write them down. We had a pretty good partnership before we wrote our stuff down and then we wrote it down and it got better. So just don't think you're too good for it. And as a side note, if you're like, man, I don't write anything down, like Alex, he's like, I knew he wouldn't do it. So instead I wrote it for him and then I showed it to him and I was like, this is so good. And then he went through and was like, yep. So if one of you doesn't wanna write it down because you're like that and you're kind of all over the place, then the other one can do it and then you review it. And then I have to own it if I don't read it like thoroughly. You know what I mean? She's like, you've agreed to this, part four. And I'm like, I read that. No, I'm kidding. But for real though, it really does matter. Second, both do the same things, slash responsibilities at different times. So like the number one thing that I hate within the gym owner space is like, two best friend personal trainers say, hey, let's start a gym together. Some of you guys are here. I don't mean it offensively. I'm just saying it's not my favorite setup. I would prefer you guys pick your dynamic and model this because if you have two people of the same skill set and they have the same responsibilities, then y'all are both doing the same thing, which means neither of you are doing it. And then one of you is inevitably gonna do more of it than the other and then you both think you're doing more than the other person and then inevitably fights just happen. It's unavoidable. So don't do that. Third, lack of trust. This is kind of more if it's not your spouse. You know, would you trust this person with your child? Right? Would you work for this person? If that person, like if the roles were reversed, if you had an operation, I would work for you all the time. I feel like I do sometimes. But like for employees, this is extra important is having that, like you need to make sure that you trust them. Fourth, which is having different mission or values. And this is really just setting the intention when you start the conversation, which is where you wanna go. Is this really a ride you wanna ride? And when we hire people, we go so hard on trying to scare them away. We're like, we do not have a work-life balance culture at Gym Launch. That's just not part of our thing. We believe that work and life go seamlessly together. And the only reason you need to balance is if you hate your work, right? If work is just living, then it's just living. And so you need to make sure that that person is 100% aligned with those values. And we both prefer to be really, really aggressive on the front and try and repel as many of those people away as possible and it's worked well. But if you're not checking for the stuff in the beginning, you're just setting yourself a time bomb for four months later. Boom. Part four. How does this apply to you if you're a solo operator? So if you're not a solo operator, do you feel like you have an understanding of what your role should be that you're gonna write them down? Yes? Okay. Yay. You gotta pause. Okay. So if you're not married to the person who's operating your business, right? First follower becomes even more important to you because if I were not married to Layla, I would need someone, I would need a Layla figure in this business or it would not work, I promise you. And it's like, Layla fulfills the role of like, almost like mother goose to like the staff. She's like that, like almost like a matriarch figure to the company. And that operation manager should be like that person who's the advocate for the staff. Like that's probably like, she advocates for our employees. And if you have someone who's advocating for your staff, they will then, if you treat them well, treat your customers well. That's where the retention piece comes into it, is having that culture. Second, within the perfect gym model, this would fit under the assistant manager slash operations manager role. If you're like unclear, like where does that person fit within the model? And just remember that clients and employees will follow the first-follower's behavior. Well, sometimes this person wear the operations manager and then like the training manager and the sales manager and kind of undulate until you fill that roll up. Yeah. Well, I would say, sorry. No, go ahead. Before we had the team that we have back there, I did everything pretty much, like all those roles. So I was like, when Matt and Dylan came on, I was managing the sales team. And then I was also doing billing. And I was also trying to do customer service. So, and then, but my goal, and like the goal of anyone that's an operations manager is as you grow, they are the one who's gonna have the bigger team. So then their main goal isn't to like hire the whole team. It's like hire the important key players and then they'll hire the rest of the team, right? So like if you have an operations manager and then it gets so big, you need to hire sales manager. Then that sales manager is the one who dictates if you need to hire anyone else below them. And then the billing manager dictates if anyone needs to get hired below them. You see, so they're just delegating authority and decision making down. And just as a piggyback on what she's saying, what we just talked about is a framework that can scale a gym to much larger than one gym, right? Because if you had a sales manager, it'd be like if you have four gyms, you'd have a salesperson at each gym. And then you have a sales manager who would be managing to make sure that they're hitting the numbers, working the leads, et cetera, within the case of one facility. Most of the time, like you can have a lead trainer if that like matters, you know what I mean, for them, which is fine. But besides the sales manager, like the operations manager is really like the GM. You know what I mean? The business manager is really running the business for the most part. If we like don't want to be at the gym, it shouldn't be one of us then. Right, yeah, if you both don't want to be at the gym, then you'd have to find someone who's going to be that right hand, like if you want to be one unit, then you'd have to have one person who would be that go to right hand, who you both trust implicitly. Yeah, we'll go there and then we'll go Carlos and then. We'll find him. Oh, that's Phil, my bad. What's going on guys? So, all right, so we have a pretty robust team and obviously we're fairly new to this game. So we've been throwing a lot of our team members into roles that we think they're supposed to be in, but obviously we're green as shit too. So it is what it is, right? I think one thing you have to think about if you're ever transferring someone somewhere is like, is it fair to them? And is it what they want? Because sometimes they'll just take the next best thing because you're putting it there and offering it. And there's been times when we've had to choose either letting someone go or demoting them. And it's almost been unfair to demote them because it would prevent them from getting further on their career path. Copy that. She came in clean in toilet, so she's moved up fairly fast. Yes. Thanks guys. Oh, got it. I got a bump finger. So is there somebody on the team that I could chat with? I would just do it to the piece that applies to the group training, because I know you have, I know that you have large, you have facility usage at your gym. But for that, like the changes that are gonna happen there, just change it there. Bumping facility usage, in my opinion, that's why I'm not in the facility usage business, is that it's a very commoditized space. It's very difficult to provide value without having a $10 million facility like Equinox, and they only get to $99 a month. You know what I mean? It's hard to play that game, but providing more value by having better service is a game I'm very happy to play. And that's, so if you're gonna make the changes, just layer it onto that side. That makes sense. So yeah, I feel like I wanna sit down with somebody. Help me figure this through. It's all good. Just pass it back there and then, what's up? So we got this awesome team that has been with us for a while now, and we joined Jim Launch 16 weeks ago. So we just changed tons in terms of volume processes, what we expected of them, our expectations went way up of them. And they don't, I'm the type of guy that wants to change, change, change, change, change, and some of our teammates are not. So how much time should we give them? So for example, we say every communication has to go through email now. We need everything through email. We need a document just like you said. And they can't respond to an email for the life of a map. A lot of them are young guys, 19, 20, 21 years old. They barely know what email is. And they just can't respond to emails. So we remind them, remind them, remind them. It's been three, four weeks of reminding them and beating these new processes into them. And they're still not responding. What do you do that we've done? Performance improvement plans? What's the consequence if they don't respond the first time? Well, I don't know. We bug them more. So I think someone that's really good at this on our team is Lily, actually, who manages customer service. And so it's like basically three strikes you're out. And you have to be serious about that. So it depends, it's like what kind of culture do you want to build? Do you want to build the kind of culture where people slack off? Or maybe you did have that kind of culture and now the kind of culture you're trying to build is one where you have more routine. And you have to have a tighter team because what you've discovered, because of Jim launch and everything that Alex is teaching you, is that in order to survive in any industry, you have to be nimble and you have to be constantly changing based on the market, right? Well, I would do my best as the leader to transfer that knowledge to them of like, listen, we're doing this so like you can all eat because my business, like this business feeds all of us, right? So it's like, that is number one. Number two is like, if we do not have the right communication line, we cannot make the business churn and we cannot make the clients happy in order to actually feed ourselves, right? So I think explaining that to them clearly is one thing. Explain to them why you make all the changes is another. Having them involved in, hey, do you guys think this is the right way to make this change? Or do you think that there's a better way to do this? Is the third thing. And then lastly, if they still don't follow through with anything and they don't have any proposed solutions, then it doesn't sound like they're a good fit for what you need moving forward. So a clear cut, almost like disciplinary procedure, if they're not following. Yeah. As a last recourse, I would do the first three things you said first though. Yeah, because you want to make sure, as long as you take ownership over like, all right, I'm gonna check off the four things that I know I could have done wrong. I'm gonna go back and look at those things, redo anything I didn't do, and that the problem persists two weeks from now, then I know it's not on me. And that's what any leader, like in our company, we see it all the time, if there's something, if there's someone struggling, that leader's gonna say, okay, well, what did I do wrong? Did I not communicate this correctly? Maybe I wasn't clear about this, maybe I didn't set proper boundaries, so I'm gonna go restructure this, and sometimes it works really well, and the person responds awesome, and sometimes that person just wasn't a good fit. It's also why having the core tenants is so huge, because we can always go back to them. I know, we didn't talk about them today. I think, are you talking about them tomorrow at all? You are, okay. But having those core tenants is so key, because they're the pillars, like they're the pillars of the values of how you make decisions. And so if it says own it, it's one of ours, and someone's not owning the outcome, they're not owning their own responsibility, they'll be like, how are you following this core value? And then it's like, well, then it becomes really clear that it's not a cultural fit anymore. So, Jason. That would be a huge cost to the business. Are you guys married? Not yet, but we're engaged, we'll be married this year. I was like, oh, I work way better when you're married. Right, yeah. So I would just, you mean like establishing what the incentives are, I don't know if that's an incentive, I think hitting X amount, and that's like what is expected as a deliverable, but I don't think something that's not tied to profit should be percentage based. So then I guess the more clear question there would be, do you go hourly or do you go salary? Cause we've gotten bitten before going salary, and then that person doesn't basically live up to that salary, and so it becomes a cost. There's so many factors when it goes into that, of like what state you live in, what they're doing on the job, like it's very HR, like heavy, cause we've gone through it like, one company's like yeah, they're salarying, the next is like they're hourly, and then I'm like, oh my God, kill me. I'm gonna be honest. So that has to do with you, I'm sorry, I didn't, he didn't tell me your name. But it has to do with your confidence really, is like do you know that you can go in there and frickin' run shit so that it is going to boost the bottom line. And so that's you being okay with the risk that like if you don't put in the work, and if you do don't have a great communication cycle, that it might not go your way. Like I knew that I was gonna leave everything behind and in a month it might fail. But you have to be okay with that. And honestly, I think that kind of shows you what you're made of in life. It's like you take the risk, and you know you have the opportunity to succeed. Like if she comes in and out of whatever, her just managing people, suddenly your profit increases by 15% a month or something like that, then you know it, it was awesome, right? But it could also go the other way.
